respStmt.name: Dirk Van Hulle
respStmt.resp: SBDMP coordinator, encoding scheme, TEI customization

respStmt.name: Vincent Neyt
respStmt.resp: Project architecture, encoding scheme, TEI customization

publicationStmt.authority: Centre for Manuscript Genetics (University of Antwerp)

availability.p: Not to be distributed outside the project

sourceDesc.p:
Abbreviated in the Manuscript Chronology as ET2
'Crapp's Last Tape'. Typed manuscript, signed, March 1958, 10 pp., 4to. With many autograph corrections, annotations, and revisions in black and blue inks. This manuscript, labeled by Beckett as 'Typescrypt II', reflects the changes he made in the first typescript (some of which have been revised again) and is a major expansion of that version, corresponding more recognizably to the published text. Introductory material relating to the stage setting has been added, and the verso of page 1 has a long autograph addition which incorporates stage directions concerning A's [Krapp's] movements. Whereas the first typescript was untitled, here Beckett has written 'Crapp's Last Tape' at the top of page 1. A mathematical calculation on the verso of the final page relates to the passage: 'To drink less, in particular. And the resolutions! (Brief laugh of A alone). Statistics. Seventeen hundred hours, out of the preceding eight thousand off, consumed on licensed premises alone. Over 20%'.
Lake, Carlton, ed. (1984), No Symbols Where None Intended: A Catalogue of Books, Manuscripts and Other Material Relating to Samuel Beckett in the Collections of the Humanities Research Center (Austin: Humanities Research Center), p. 105

head.add: Typescript II of CRAPP'S LAST TAPEcentred

stage.seg: The first sentences are typed on the back of the typescript\'s first page. After this false start Beckett apparently took out the sheet from the typewriter, and started again on the other side. Front centre,

sitting on a small plain wooden chair before
a small plain wooden table, X.

p.seg:
Front centre, sitting ⁁
left profile to public,
facing front on a small wooden chair before at a
xx
small narrow wp ooden table, ⁁
the two drawers of which xx the two drawers of which open towards audience insertion mark turned into arrow pointing to addition in the top margin facing front X.
Little wearish old man.
Rusty nar black narrow trousers, too short for him.
Rusty black sleeveless waistoca ot, four capacious pockets.

Heavy
silver watch and chain.

Grimy white shirt, open at neck.
No collar.

Surprising pair of dirty white boots, size ten, very
narrow and pointed.
Pallid face.
Purple nose.
Very near-sighted (but unspectacled) and
hard of hearing.
Croaking Cracked voice,
characteristic peculiar accent.

On the centre of table a tape-recorder. and On its right a number of cardboard
boxes containing recorded tapes.
⁁ On its left A thick worn ledger.
Table and immedit aely adjacent area in strong white light.

Rest
of stage in darkness.

add.note: Insertion point marked by a capital A, referring to a substantial addition on the verso

seg.stage: (reading from ledger, his nose down on it).

add.note: The following addition is written in black ink on the back of the typescript\'s first page.
add.seg:
Crapp sits a moment motionless, looks at his watch, feels in his
waistcoat pockets, takes out a small bunch takes out an envelope,
puts it back, feels, takes out a small bunch of keys, h
raises it close to his eyes, chooses a key, gets up and moves to
front of table.

He stoops, unlocks a drawer, peers into it,
feels about inside it, closes it, locks it, opens the other,
peers into it, feels about inside, takes out a large banana . ,
He locks draw closes and locks drawer, puts keys back
in his pocket.

He turns, advances to edge of stage, halts, shows the audience the
holds out banana for audience to see,
peels i t banana
banana,
droops drops
the skin, bites off the end
sucks the end
puts the end of the banana in his mouth, staring
vacuously before him.

He turns aside and starts
walking to and fro at edge of stage, eating banana
and meditating x in light, i.e. not more than
4 paces 4 or five paces in either direction, eating
banana and meditation.

He treads on skin, slips,
nearly falls, recovers himself, pushes skin with his
foot over edge of stage into pit,

resumes his pacing,
finishes banana, retu returns to table, opens wrong takes keys from his pocket, raises them to his eyes,
chooses key, un unlocks and opens wrong drawer, peers and feels inside,
locks it, unlocks and opens other drawer, takes out a
second banana, locks drawer, x puts keys back in his pocket,
turns, advances to x edge of stage, peels banana, puts skin
in his pocket, puts the end of banana in his mouth, staring
vacuously before him.

He has a sudden idea, drops banana
at his feet, turns and goes backstage into dark.

He comes back
into light, sits down at table. wiping his mouth, sits down
at table.

He wipes his mouth, wipes his hands
on his waistcoat, brings them smartly together,
rubs them them together.

stage.hi:
(He bends over ledger, turns the pages,
finds the entry he wants, reads.)

p.seg:
Box...thrree...
spool...five.
p.stage:
( He turns his head and stares front.
With
relish.)
p.seg: Spool!
Spoool!
p.stage:
( Pause.
He turns back to
table, starts peering and poking at the boxes.)
p.seg:
Box...thrr
thrree...thrree...four...two...(with surprise) nine!..good
God! Jesus!...seven...ah!..the little rascal!

seg.stage:
(He separates box
three from the others.)

p.seg: Box thrree.

seg.stage:
(He opens it, peers at
the spools inside.)

p.seg:
Spool...(he peers at ledger, finds the
entry) f ...five. (He peers at spools.) Five...five...

seg.stage:
(He takes
out a spool, straightens up, holds up spool between finger
and thumb.)

p.seg: Spool five.

stage.seg:
(He lays it on table, closes box
three and puts it back with the others. He takes up spool.)

p.seg: Box thrree, spool five.

stage.seg:
(He puts spool on machine, peers
at ledger. Reading entry at foot of page.)

p.seg:
Mother at
rest...
The black ball...

Black
ball?
p.stage:
( Pause.)
He peers again at ledger, reads.)
p.seg:
The dark
nurse.

seg.stage: (He raises his head, broods, peers again at ledger.)

p.seg: Improvement of bowel condition...
memorale Memorable...what?
Equinox, memorable equinox.
p.stage:
( He raises
his head, stares front.
Puzzled.)
p.seg: Memorable equinox?
p.stage:
( Pause.
He shrugs his shoulders, peers again at ledger.)
p.seg:
Farewell 
to - (he turns the page
                              s
                           
                        ) - love.

He broods, closes ledger, bends over machine, switches it
on and assumes listening posture, ; i.e. ear cupped towards
machine, face front . ear pulled with leaning forward, elbows on table, ear pulled with forefinger toward hand cupping ear, face front.

seg.stage:
(strong young voice, rather pompous, but obviously his A's
at a much earlier time.)

p.seg: Thirty-seven today, and sound -

seg.stage:
(Settling himself more comfortably he A knocks a box off
the table.Instead of underlining the stage direction Beckett mistakenly typed four dashes. He ca urses, switches off machine, sweeps boxes

and ledger violently from table, switches on machine,
resumes his pose.) Beckett typed this line and a half on the other side of the sheet (2v), stopped, pulled it out of the typewriter and started again on what is now called the recto side (2r).
and ledger violently from table, windx s tape back to be
ginning, swithc switches on machine, resumes pose.)

p.seg:
Thirty-
seven today,and sound apparently in wind and limb, ↑ as a whistle, apart
from the old trouble, and intellectually I have little doubt
at the peak of my powers, or thereabouts.

Celebrated the
awful occasion, as in recent years, quietly at the Winehouse.
No face there I knew.

Sprawled before in front of the fire with closed
eyes, separating the grain from the chaff.

Jotted down a
few notes, on the back of an
old
envelope.

Good to be home
again, in my old rags.

The new light above my table is a
great improvement.

With all this darkness round me I feel
less alone.
In a way.

I liked to get up
and move about in it, then back here to...(hesitates)...
me.

The grain, what do I mean by that, I mean...(Pause.) I
suppose I mean those things worth having when all the
dust has - when all this my dust has settled.

I close my
eyes and try and to imagine them.
Pause.

Extraordinary silence this evening. I strain my ears and
do not hear r a sound.

Old Miss Beamish Hare always sings at this
hour.
Songs of her child girlhood, she says.

I admire her
But not tonight.

Shall I sing when I am her
age, if I ever am?
Did I sing as a boy?
No.

Did
I ever sing. ?
No.
Pause.
I have just listened to an old year, passages at random.

I did not check in the book, but it must be at least ten
or twelve years ago.

At that time O I was still living on and off
with Furry Bianca ⁁ in Trafalgar \'Trafalgar\' underscored with an undulating line in black ink. Street.
Well out of that.

Hopeless
business.

Not much reference to her. A queer passage about
her eyes.
I suddenly saw them ag gain.
Matchless.
Ah well.

These old P.M.s are gruesome, but I -
(A switches off, broods, switches on again) - always find them
a help before embarking on a new -er - conspectus new...
(hesitates) ... conspectus.

Hard to believe I was ever that
young pup young pup.

seg.stage:
(Bried f laugh in which
A joins.)

p.seg: To drink less, in particular.
Statistice s.

Seven
teen hundred hours, out of the preceding eight thousand odd,
consumed on licensed premises alone.

Plans for a less wear
ing sexual life.
Last illness of his father.

Flagging pur
suit of happiness suit of happiness
3 suit of happiness.
Unattainable laxation.

Shadow of
the magnum opus.

Closing with - (brief laugh) - a yelp
to Providence.

What
remains of all that misery?

A girl in a shabby green coat,
on a railway-station platform?
Pause.
When I look -
p.stage:
( A switches off machine, broods, looks at
watch, gets up and goes backstage into darkness.
Ten
seconds.
Pop of cork.
Ten seconds.
Second cork.
Ten seconds.
Third cork.
Ten seconds. ⁁
Brief rau burst of raucous song.⁁
He comes back into light front
right, sits down, switches on machine, resumes his pose.
)
p.seg:
- back on over the year that is gone, with what I hope are / vertical line connecting addition perhaps the
old eyes to come, there is of course the house on the canal
where mother lay a-dying, in the early autumn, after her
long viduity (A gives a start), and the - (A switches off
machine, gets up, goes backstage into darkness and comes
same way with a dictionary
(A givesa gives a start), and the -

seg.stage:
(A switches off machine,
wind backs winds back tape a little winds
back tape a little, bends his ear closer to machine)

p.seg:
-
a-dying, in the early autumn, after her long viduity, and
the -← arrow pointing to addition in the left margin

p.stage:
( A switches off machine, turns face front.
Puzzled.)

p.seg: Viduity?
p.stage:
( Pause.
He gets up, goes backstage into darkness,
comes back with a large dictionary volume of the Concise
Oxford or Johnson's dictionary and quotes example, lays it on the table, sits down, looks up viduity,
xeads reads, nods,
closes dictionary, switches on machine, re
sumes his pose.)

p.seg:
- ba ench by the weir from where I d could
see her window.
There I sat, da wishing she were gone.

Quite a number of people I got to know then, oh I mean by
appearance, nursemaids, children, i old men, dogs.

One dark
young beauty I recall recollect particularly, all white and starch, splendid bosom, ⁁ with a big black hooded
pram.

Whenever I looked in her direction she had her eyes
on me.

But when I was foolish enough to speak to her she
threatened to call a policeman.
The face she had!
Eyes

like
...moonstones.

8 I was there when - (A switches off, broods,
switches on again) - the blind went down, one of those
dirty brown roller affairs, throwing a ball for a dog as
it happened. chance wd. have it.

I happened to happened Beckett crossed out \'happened to\' (because of the repetition; cf. previous sentence) and added \'ed\' to \'look\'; then changed \'it happened\' in the previous sentence into \'chance wd. have it\' and added \'happened\' again, without bothering to undo the previous changes. looked up and there it was.

Over
at last.

I sat on for a moment or two few moments with the ball in my hand
hand and the dog barking and pawing at me.

Moments
Moments.
Her moments. My moments.

In the
end I held it out to him and he took it in his mouth, gently
gently.

An old tennis ball, black and sodden, but unpunct
ured.
I wonder will that mean something some day.
Pause.

Intellectually a year of profound gloom and indigence until
that memorable night in March, at the end of the pier, in
the howling wind, never to be forgotten, when suddenly I
saw the whole thing.
The turning-point, at last.

This I
imagine is what I hac ve chiefly to set down this evening,
against the day when my work will be done and perhaps no
x4
place left in my memory, and no thankfulness, for the
miracle that - - ( pause) - for r the fire that set it alight.

What I saw then was that the assumption I had been going on
all my life, namely -

seg.stage:
(A switches off impatiently,
winds tapes forward, switches on again)

p.seg:
- granite rocks the
foam flying up in the light of the beacon and the anemometer
spinning like a propellor, clear to me at last that g the
dae rk I have struggled to keep at bay been fighting off all this time is in reality my most -

seg.stage:
(A curses, switches off, winds tapes forward, switches on
again)

p.seg:
- unshatterable association till my dying day of
storm and night with the light of the understanding and -

seg.stage:
(A curses louder, switches switches off, winds tape violently for
ward, switches on again)

p.seg:
- my face in her breat sts and my
hand on her.
We lay there without moving.

But underneath us
all moved, and moved us, gently
sofly
gently, up and down, and from side
to side.
Pause.
Past midnight.
Never knew such a silence.
Pause.
Here I end this -
He A switches off, winds tape back, switches on again.

- upper lake, with the punt, bathed off the bank, then
pushed out into the stream and drifted.

She lay stretcg hed
out on the floorboards with her hands under her head and
her eyes closed.

Blazing Sun blazing down, bit of a breeze,
and the water nice and lively.

I noticed a scratch on her
thigh and asked her how she got it.

Picking gooseberries,
she said.

I said again I thought it was hopeless and no
good going on, and she agreed, without opening her eyes.

I asked her to look at me and after a few moments - (pause)
- after a few moments she did, but the eyes just slits,
because of the glare.

I bent over her to get them in the
shadowed and they opened.
Let me in.
We drifted in among the reeds and stuck.

The way they bent,
sighing, before the stem.

I lay down across her with my
face in her breasts and my hand on her.

We lay without mov
ing.

But underneath us all moved, and moved us, ⁁
gently
sofly
gently, up and down,
and from side to side.
Pause.
Past midnight.
Never knew -

seg.hi: A switches off, sits brooding broods,

fumbles in his waistcoat
pockets, tal kes out an om ld envelope, fumbles, puts back the
envelope, looks at his watch, gets up and goes

Sound of bottle againt
against glass, then brief siphon.

p.seg:
He comes back front
left into light, with an unused tape in his hand, sits
down, takes tape off machine, lays it on table, puts the
5
other tape on machine, takes envelope from his pocket, m lays it Beckett typed this line and a half on the other side of the sheet (5v), stopped, pulled it out of the typewriter and started again on what is now called the recto side (5r).
other tape on machine, takes envelope from his pocket,
lays it on the table, switches on and begins to record.

Just been listening to that stupid bastard I was thirty
years ago, find it hard to believe I was ever as bad as
that.
The eyes she had!
p.stage:
( Broods, realizes he is recording
q silence, switches off, broods.)
p.seg:
Everything g there, every
thing,on earth all the -

seg.stage:
(Realizes this is not being re
corded, switches on.)

p.seg:
Everything there, everything on eart
on earth, all the light and dark and vice and crystal and
sor rrow and rejoicing of -
hunger & feasting crapulence of...(hesitates)...the ages!

Let that
go!
Jesus!
Take his mind off his homework!
Jesus!
Ah well, maybe he was right.

Maybe he was
right.
p.stage:
( Broods.
Realizes.
Switches off.
Consults envelope.
Crumples it r and throws it away.
Broods.
Switches on.
)
p.seg: Nothing to say, nothing.
What's a year now?

Reverie and
galloping constipation irreversible constipation.

Revelled
in the word spool.
Spoool!

High
light Happiest moment of the year.

Seventeen copies sold, of which
eleven in Tanganyika eleven in Tanganyika overseas.
Getting known.

One pound
six and something, eight no doubt.

Crawled out once or t
twice, before the summer was over.

Sat in the park in the
middle of the brats and skivvies, dreaming and wishing I
were gone.
Last fancies.
Swore to fight them off.

Aspir
ations.
Resolutions.

Burnt Scalded the eyes out of me reading
Effie again, a page a day, with tears again.
Effie...

Could have been happy wo ith her, up there on the
cold sea, and the pines, and the dunes.

Fanny
came in a couple of times.
B Bony old whore.

Couldn't do
much, but better than nothing.
The last time wasn't so bad.
W How do you manage it, she said, at your age?

I told her I'd
been saving up for her all my life.

Went to Vespers
p once, like when I was a child.

seg.add:
Now the day is over
Night is drawing nigh-igh,
Shadows of the evening,
Steal across the sky.
Went to sleep and fell off the pew.

p.seg:
Sometimesa
wondered in the night if a last effort mightn't -
Empty the bottle now and get to your bed.

Finish this pukx e ½
tomorrow.
Or leave it at that.
Leave it at that.
Lie propped up on my back in the dark - and wander.

Be again in the dingle on a Christmas Eve, picking gathering
holly.

Be again on Croghan on a Sunday morning, in
the snow haze, with the bitch, stop and listent to the bells.
And so on.
Be again.

All that
old misery.
Once wasn't enough.

Lie down
across he. r.
p.stage:
( Long pause.
He suddenly bends to the machine,
switches off, wrenches off tape, throws it away, puts on
the other, winds it to the passage he wants, switches on,
listens staring front. )

p.seg: - gooseberries, she said.

I said again I thought it was
hopeless and no good going on, and she agreed, without
opening her eyes.

I asked her to ll ook at me and after a
few moments - (pause) - after a few moments she did, but
the eyes just slits, because of the glare.

I bent over her ½
6
to get them in the shadow and they opened.
And l Let me in.

We drifted in among the reeds and
stuck.
They way they bent, sighing, before the stem.

I lay
down across her with my face in her breasts and my hand on
her.
We lay without moving.

But underneath us all moved,
and moved us, gently
sofly
gently, up and down, and from side to side.
No sound.
Past midnih gt.
Never knew such silence.
Pause.
Here I end this...(hesitates)...stage spool,

B box - (pause) -
thrree, spool number - (pause) - five.

Perhaps my best
years are past gone.
When there was a chance of happiness.

But
I wouldn't want them back over again.
Not with the fire in me now.

No,
I wouldn't want them back. over again.

seg.hi: The tape runs on in silence

p.seg: Curtain centred

p.add:
for
Jake Schwartz
Sam Beckett
March 1958
